event: Neolithic farming communities along the Yellow and Yangtze rivers begin cultivating millet and rice, laying the foundations of Chinese civilization.
The Yangshao culture flourishes in the central Yellow River valley, producing distinctive painted pottery.
The Longshan culture develops advanced black pottery, walled settlements, and early social stratification.
Traditional dating of the founding of the Xia dynasty, regarded as China's first hereditary dynasty.
The Shang dynasty rises, leaving the earliest Chinese writing on oracle bones and sophisticated bronze casting.
The Zhou dynasty overthrows the Shang and introduces the concept of the Mandate of Heaven to justify rule.
Western Zhou collapses after a nomadic attack, beginning the Eastern Zhou and the era of declining royal power.
The philosopher Confucius is born, founding a tradition of ethics and governance that shapes Chinese thought for millennia.
The Warring States period begins as rival states fight for supremacy amid major military and philosophical innovation.
Qin Shi Huang unifies China and proclaims himself First Emperor, founding the Qin dynasty and a centralized imperial state.
Qin Shi Huang connects and extends earlier walls into an early version of the Great Wall to guard the northern frontier.
The Qin dynasty collapses after the emperor's death, leading to civil war among contending warlords.
Liu Bang founds the Han dynasty, inaugurating a golden age of Chinese imperial culture and administration.
Emperor Wu sends Zhang Qian westward, opening the Silk Road trade routes linking China to Central Asia and beyond.
Wang Mang usurps the throne and establishes the short-lived Xin dynasty, interrupting Han rule.
The Eastern Han dynasty is restored with its capital at Luoyang.
The court official Cai Lun improves and standardizes papermaking, a transformative Chinese invention.
The Han dynasty falls, fragmenting China into the rival Three Kingdoms of Wei, Shu, and Wu.
The Sui dynasty reunifies a long-divided China and begins constructing the Grand Canal.
The Tang dynasty is founded, ushering in a cosmopolitan golden age of poetry, art, and territorial expansion.
Wu Zetian declares herself emperor, becoming the only woman to rule China in her own name.
The An Lushan Rebellion erupts, devastating the Tang dynasty and weakening central authority.
The Tang collapses, fracturing China during the turbulent Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period.
The Song dynasty is established, presiding over commercial growth, urbanization, and the spread of printing.
The Jurchen Jin capture the Song capital, forcing the dynasty south to establish the Southern Song.
Kublai Khan proclaims the Yuan dynasty, beginning Mongol rule over all of China.
Zhu Yuanzhang expels the Mongols and founds the Ming dynasty, restoring native Chinese rule.
Admiral Zheng He launches the first of seven grand maritime expeditions across the Indian Ocean.
The Ming dynasty falls and the Manchu-led Qing dynasty seizes Beijing, founding China's last imperial dynasty.
The Qing conquers Taiwan, incorporating the island into imperial administration.
The First Opium War breaks out as Britain forces China open to the opium trade and foreign commerce.
The Treaty of Nanjing cedes Hong Kong to Britain and opens treaty ports, beginning China's 'century of humiliation'.
The Taiping Rebellion erupts, becoming one of the deadliest civil wars in history before its suppression in 1864.
The First Sino-Japanese War begins, ending in defeat and the loss of Taiwan to Japan in 1895.
The Boxer Rebellion against foreign influence is crushed by an international expeditionary force.
The Xinhai Revolution overthrows the Qing dynasty, ending more than two thousand years of imperial rule.
The Republic of China is established with Sun Yat-sen as provisional president.
The May Fourth Movement ignites nationalist and cultural reform protests against foreign concessions.
The Chinese Communist Party is founded in Shanghai.
Communist forces begin the Long March, a strategic retreat that elevates Mao Zedong to leadership.
Japan launches a full-scale invasion, beginning the Second Sino-Japanese War with atrocities such as the Nanjing Massacre.
Japan surrenders at the end of World War II, and Taiwan is returned to Chinese control.
Mao Zedong proclaims the People's Republic of China after Communist victory, while the Nationalists retreat to Taiwan.
The Great Leap Forward begins, triggering a catastrophic famine that kills tens of millions.
Mao launches the Cultural Revolution, unleashing a decade of political upheaval and persecution.
The People's Republic of China takes China's seat at the United Nations, replacing the Republic of China.
U.S. President Richard Nixon visits China, beginning the normalization of relations between the two powers.
Deng Xiaoping launches market-oriented economic reforms and the policy of opening up to the world.
Pro-democracy protests in Tiananmen Square are suppressed by the military, drawing international condemnation.
Hong Kong is returned to Chinese sovereignty under the 'one country, two systems' framework.
China joins the World Trade Organization, accelerating its integration into the global economy.
Beijing hosts the Summer Olympic Games, showcasing China's rising global stature.
China overtakes Japan to become the world's second-largest economy.
President Xi Jinping launches the Belt and Road Initiative to expand global infrastructure and influence.
The COVID-19 pandemic emerges from Wuhan, reshaping global health and China's relations with the world.
